A-005-001-001: A Hartley oscillator uses inductive feedback: its resonant coil is tapped, and the tap feeds part of the signal back to the input in phase to sustain oscillation. The tell-tale of a Hartley is this tapped inductor, whereas a Colpitts uses a capacitive divider for feedback; link coupling and a neutralizing capacitor do not set the feedback.
A-005-001-002: A Colpitts oscillator uses a capacitive voltage divider (two series capacitors across the tank) to tap off the feedback signal, the capacitive counterpart of the Hartley's tapped coil. Remember Colpitts and capacitor both start with C. A tapped coil would make it a Hartley instead.
A-005-001-003: A Pierce oscillator couples feedback through capacitance, with a quartz crystal acting as the high-Q resonant element for excellent stability. It is essentially a Colpitts arrangement built around a crystal, so the feedback path is capacitive. A tapped coil (Hartley) or neutralizing capacitor would not describe it.
A-005-001-004: The Colpitts is favoured for a variable-frequency oscillator because its capacitive feedback gives good frequency stability over a tuning range. Stability is the prized trait in a VFO so the transmitted frequency does not drift. It is a free-running LC oscillator, not crystal-controlled, and oscillators are kept low-power buffered stages, so high output power is not the reason.
A-005-001-005: In a PLL synthesizer the output frequency is locked to and multiplied from the reference, so any phase jitter in the reference is faithfully transferred and even magnified, appearing as phase noise on the output. This is why a clean, stable reference (often a crystal) is essential. The distractors confuse amplitude variation with phase, but a PLL locks on phase, not amplitude.
A-005-001-006: Feedback taken from a capacitive divider (two series capacitors across the tank) is the defining feature of the Colpitts oscillator. A Hartley would use a tapped coil instead, and a Miller uses a different crystal arrangement. Match capacitive to Colpitts (both start with C).
A-005-001-007: Silver mica capacitors are chosen for the feedback divider in a high-stability oscillator because they have very low loss, tight tolerance, and excellent temperature stability, so the frequency does not drift. Electrolytics are polarized, lossy, and only suit power supply filtering, while ceramic and Mylar drift more with temperature. Stability of the frequency-determining parts is everything in an oscillator.
A-005-001-008: When feedback is obtained through a single capacitor in series with the crystal, the circuit is a Pierce oscillator, which relies on the crystal as its resonant element. The series capacitor lets you trim the exact operating frequency. A Colpitts uses two capacitors as a divider, and Hartley uses a tapped coil.
A-005-001-009: An oscillator is the one circuit here that depends on positive (regenerative) feedback to sustain a continuous output, so a variable-frequency oscillator is correct. Mixers, detectors, and audio amplifiers process or amplify signals but do not require positive feedback to function. Positive feedback is the hallmark of any oscillator.
A-005-001-010: An oscillator to set the frequency followed by a class C amplifier to boost power is the classic make-up of a simple two-stage CW (Morse) transmitter. Class C is highly efficient but non-linear, so it suits CW or FM but would distort SSB. There is no modulator or sideband generator described, ruling out the SSB and FM choices.
A-005-001-011: Feedback through a single capacitor in series with the crystal identifies a Pierce oscillator, which uses the crystal directly as its frequency-controlling resonator. A Colpitts uses a two-capacitor divider, a Hartley a tapped coil, and a Franklin a two-stage design. The crystal-plus-series-capacitor signature points to Pierce.
A-005-002-001: The adjustable pi network at a power amplifier output is an impedance-matching circuit: tuning it transforms the antenna impedance to the value the amplifier needs for maximum, efficient power transfer. It also helps filter harmonics, but its primary job is matching. It does not switch antennas or perform frequency multiplication.
A-005-002-002: Returning the filament transformer secondary at its centre tap balances the AC heater supply so that 60 Hz hum is not impressed on the cathode and thus does not modulate the RF output. Without this balanced return the AC filament voltage would add unwanted hum to the emitted signal. The benefit is clean emission, not harmonic suppression or output power.
A-005-002-003: In a grounded-grid triode amplifier the grid is at RF ground and the input signal is applied to the cathode, while output is taken from the plate. This configuration provides good stability and often needs no neutralization. Applying signal to the plate or grid would describe other configurations, not grounded grid.
A-005-002-004: A blocking (coupling) capacitor connects the plate to the pi-network: it passes the RF output while blocking the high DC plate voltage from reaching the tuning network and antenna. A bypass capacitor would shunt RF to ground rather than couple it onward. So a DC-blocking capacitor is correct.
A-005-002-005: The plate is fed its DC high voltage through an RF choke, and the far end of that choke connects to B+ (the high-voltage supply). The choke passes DC to the plate while blocking RF from entering the power supply. It is the high-voltage feed, not filament, ground, or bias.
A-005-002-006: In a grounded-grid stage the cathode receives the input, and an RF choke in the cathode circuit returns to ground (B-), completing the DC path while keeping RF out of that return. The choke passes the cathode DC current to ground but blocks the RF signal. Hence the other end goes to B- / ground, not to B+ or the filament supply.
A-005-002-007: A transformer secondary wired directly to the tube's heater pins supplies the low-voltage filament (heater) voltage that warms the cathode for electron emission. High voltage (B+) is fed through a choke to the plate, not directly from a transformer to the tube pins. So this transformer provides filament voltage.
A-005-002-008: Efficiency = RF output power / DC input power, so DC input = output / efficiency = 400 W / 0.50 = 800 W. With plate current of 400 mA = 0.4 A, B+ = power / current = 800 / 0.4 = 2000 volts. Forgetting to divide by efficiency would wrongly give 1000 V.
A-005-002-009: Each filament lead is grounded for RF through a bypass capacitor, which shunts any RF on the heater line to ground while leaving the DC/AC filament supply undisturbed. This keeps RF out of the filament wiring in a grounded-grid amplifier. They are bypass capacitors, not tuning, electrolytic, or blocking types.
A-005-002-010: Before powering up after internal tuning, make certain all shielding is reinstalled and fastened: the shields protect you from lethal high voltage and RF burns and contain RF to prevent interference. Never bypass safety interlocks or run with shields off. Safety always comes first with high-power amplifiers.
A-005-002-011: Harmonics created early in a transmitter are reduced later by tuned-circuit (resonant) coupling between stages, since each tuned circuit favours the desired frequency and rejects the unwanted harmonics. Bigger coupling capacitors pass more of everything, including harmonics, so they do not help. Selective tuned coupling is the filtering mechanism.
A-005-003-001: Besides coupling energy from the oscillator to the class C amplifier, the RF transformer's winding works with capacitance to form a tuned (resonant) circuit, selecting the desired frequency. Inductive coupling alone transfers power, but its inductance also becomes part of a tuned circuit. It is not providing oscillator feedback here, which happens within the oscillator stage itself.
A-005-003-002: An RF choke in series with grounded (bypass) capacitors forms a low-pass filter that feeds DC to the collector while keeping RF out of the power supply line. The choke passes DC but blocks RF, and the capacitors shunt any remaining RF to ground. This is a supply-decoupling low-pass arrangement, not a key-click or feedback circuit.
A-005-003-003: In a two-stage CW transmitter the first stage is the oscillator and the second stage amplifies that signal to a usable level, so the second transistor acts as a power amplifier. The oscillator sets the frequency; the second stage simply boosts power. It is not a multiplier or oscillator in this simple design.
A-005-003-004: Keying a buffer (or amplifier) stage rather than the oscillator itself keeps the oscillator running continuously, so it does not have to restart on each key-down and is far less likely to shift frequency (chirp). Letting the oscillator free-run gives a stable, clean note. The other options misstate the effect on clicks, bandwidth, and voltages.
A-005-003-005: An amplifier is properly neutralized when changing or tuning the output (plate) circuit causes minimum change in grid current, showing no RF is feeding back from output to input. Residual feedback would make grid current jump as you tune the plate. So you adjust for the least change in grid current, not the least or most grid current itself.
A-005-003-006: Neutralization deliberately feeds back a small out-of-phase signal that cancels the unwanted positive feedback caused by the tube's internal capacitance, preventing self-oscillation. In short, it cancels the effect of positive feedback. It has nothing to do with power-supply hum, grid modulation, or differential gain.
A-005-003-007: The final amplifier is neutralized to eliminate parasitic (self-sustained) oscillations caused by internal feedback, which would otherwise generate spurious signals and instability. Cancelling that feedback keeps the stage a clean amplifier. Neutralization does not set modulation index, standby cutoff, or carrier frequency.
A-005-003-008: Parasitic oscillations arise from accidental, unintended resonant frequencies formed by stray inductance and capacitance in the power amplifier, often well away from the operating frequency. These unplanned resonances let the stage self-oscillate. They are not the harmonics, drive level, or line mismatch of the operating signal.
A-005-003-009: Parasitic oscillations occur mostly in RF power output stages, where high gain at RF plus stray reactances can create unintended resonant feedback loops. Audio output and rectifier stages do not operate at the RF frequencies where these spurious resonances form. High-gain RF power amplifiers are the usual offenders.
A-005-003-010: Some vacuum tubes have enough interelectrode (plate-to-grid) capacitance to feed output back to the input and cause oscillation; neutralization cancels this feedback to stop it. The cure addresses the internal capacitance, not grid leakage, filament hum, or loaded Q. So neutralization fixes capacitance-driven oscillation.
A-005-003-011: Parasitic oscillations in an RF power amplifier are commonly caused by lack of (or inadequate) neutralization, which lets internal feedback drive the stage into unwanted self-oscillation. Proper neutralization cancels that feedback. Overdrive, regulation, and harmonics are separate problems, not the typical cause of parasitics.
A-005-004-001: A balanced modulator multiplies the audio and carrier in a way that cancels the carrier itself, leaving both sidebands, so its output is double-sideband suppressed carrier (DSB-SC). A following filter then removes one sideband to make SSB. By design it nulls the carrier, so full-carrier and single-sideband-direct answers are wrong.
A-005-004-002: SSB is generated by the filter method: a balanced modulator produces a double-sideband suppressed-carrier signal, then a sharp filter removes one sideband, leaving a single sideband. The balanced modulator kills the carrier and the filter selects the wanted sideband. A reactance modulator produces FM, not SSB.
A-005-004-003: The carrier is suppressed in the balanced modulator stage, where the carrier and audio combine so the carrier components cancel, leaving only the sidebands. The mechanical filter then removes one sideband but does not suppress the carrier. So carrier suppression happens in the balanced modulator.
A-005-004-004: Compared with AM, SSB drops the carrier and one redundant sideband: removing the second sideband saves about 3 dB and removing the carrier about another 3 dB, for roughly a 6 dB advantage at the transmitter. At the receiver, SSB’s roughly half-as-wide bandwidth passes about half the noise, adding about a 3 dB advantage. This is the basis for the familiar figure that SSB is about 9 dB better than AM overall; the option claiming SSB needs more receiver bandwidth is wrong.
A-005-004-005: When an SSB transmitter is driven by two equal test tones, the standard figure for the two-tone test is that the peak envelope power is twice the power of a single tone. The two tones add at the envelope peaks to give a power above either tone alone, so the 'equal to' and 'one-half' options are wrong.
A-005-004-006: Two audio sine waves (a two-tone test) are fed in so that the SSB output forms a clean crisscross envelope on a scope; any flattening or distortion of that pattern reveals non-linearity. A single tone produces a steady carrier-like output that cannot show linearity, and speech is too irregular to judge. Two tones are the standard.
A-005-004-007: Two non-harmonically related audio tones are fed into the mic input and the transmitter output is viewed on an oscilloscope, where the envelope pattern reveals any flat-topping or distortion. They must be non-harmonic so their products do not overlap and mask distortion. A distortion analyzer is not how this visual envelope test is read.
A-005-004-008: Any two tones may be used provided they fall within the transmitter's audio passband and are not harmonically related, so their intermodulation products are distinct and reveal distortion. Harmonically related tones would let products fall on top of each other and hide problems. There is no single required pair like 20 Hz/20 kHz.
A-005-004-009: A two-tone test viewed on an oscilloscope measures the amplifier's linearity by showing whether the output envelope stays clean or distorts (flat-tops). It is an amplitude/distortion check on an SSB stage. Frequency deviation and modulation percentage apply to FM, not to an SSB linearity test.
A-005-004-010: In a properly designed SSB transmitter the carrier is suppressed at least 40 dB below peak output, so almost none of the wasted carrier remains. A higher number means better suppression, and 40 dB is the accepted minimum standard. The no-more-than 20/30 dB answers describe inadequate suppression.
A-005-004-011: Flat-topping is distortion caused by driving an SSB transmitter too hard, so the output envelope is clipped flat at the peaks and splatter is produced on adjacent frequencies. The cause is excessive drive, not too little current. Properly suppressed carrier or correctly adjusted ALC would prevent it, so those are not what flat-topping means.
A-005-005-001: Modulation index for FM is the peak deviation divided by the modulating frequency: index = 3000 Hz / 1000 Hz = 3. Both quantities are in hertz so the result is a pure number. The answers 0.3 or 3000 come from dividing the wrong way or forgetting to divide.
A-005-005-002: Modulation index = peak deviation / modulating frequency = 6 kHz / 2 kHz = 3. Keep both in the same units (kHz here) and the result is dimensionless. Reversing the ratio gives the wrong 0.333.
A-005-005-003: Deviation ratio = maximum deviation / maximum modulating frequency = 5 kHz / 3 kHz = 1.66. Use the maximum swing (±5 kHz means 5 kHz) over the highest audio frequency. Inverting the ratio would wrongly give 0.6.
A-005-005-004: Deviation ratio = maximum deviation / maximum modulating frequency = 7.5 kHz / 3.5 kHz = 2.14. Take the peak swing of 7.5 kHz over the top audio rate of 3.5 kHz. Reversing it gives the incorrect 0.47.
A-005-005-005: With no modulation the FM carrier sits at its resting frequency, called the centre (rest) frequency, and modulation makes it swing above and below this point. Deviation and frequency shift describe the swing away from centre, not the unmodulated value. So the unmodulated carrier frequency is the centre frequency.
A-005-005-006: In FM the amount of deviation depends solely on the amplitude (loudness) of the modulating signal, not its frequency: louder audio pushes the carrier farther from centre. The modulating frequency instead determines how fast the carrier swings, not how far. So amplitude alone sets the deviation.
A-005-005-007: Frequency modulation with a single tone theoretically produces an infinite number of sideband pairs (described by Bessel functions), though only those with significant amplitude matter in practice. This is unlike AM, which makes just one pair per tone. So the correct count is infinite, not two or four.
A-005-005-008: One type of deviation meter increases modulation until a Bessel carrier null occurs, then computes deviation by multiplying the modulating frequency by the known modulation index at that null. The carrier disappears at specific index values, giving a precise calibration point. The other options misdescribe this null-based method.
A-005-005-009: Because the carrier-null method computes deviation as modulating frequency times modulation index, you must know the modulating frequency you are feeding in to use such a meter. The index value is fixed by which null you observe, so the unknown you supply is the modulating frequency. The IF filter passband is not what you need to know.
A-005-005-010: Carson's rule gives the significant bandwidth as 2 × (peak deviation + modulating frequency) = 2 × (5 kHz + 3 kHz) = 2 × 8 = 16 kHz. Add deviation and audio frequency first, then double. Forgetting to double or to add the audio gives the smaller wrong answers.
A-005-005-011: The reactance modulator runs at 12.21 MHz but the final output is 146.52 MHz, a multiplication factor of 146.52 / 12.21 = 12. Frequency multiplication multiplies deviation by the same factor, so the oscillator's deviation must be the final ±5 kHz divided by 12 = ±416.7 Hz. So the low-frequency stage deviates only ±416.7 Hz.
A-005-006-001: When two transmitters' signals mix in a non-linear final amplifier and produce radiated sum and difference frequencies, this is intermodulation interference. The mixing creates new spurious frequencies not present in either original signal. It is not simple adjacent-channel interference or desensitization, which are different effects.
A-005-006-002: Intermodulation typically happens when two repeaters are physically close, so each transmitter's signal enters the other's final amplifier and the non-linear stage mixes them into new sum and difference products. Proximity plus a non-linear amplifier is the recipe. Aircraft reflections and feedback are not the usual mechanism.
A-005-006-003: A terminated circulator or ferrite isolator in the transmission line passes the transmitter's outgoing power but absorbs signals coming back in, so the other transmitter's signal cannot reach the final amplifier and mix. This blocks the reverse path that causes intermod. Simple high- or low-pass filters do not stop on-band signals from a nearby transmitter on the same band.
A-005-006-004: Third-order intermod products fall at 2f1 - f2 and 2f2 - f1. Using 146.52 and the unknown x with the receiver at 146.70: 2 × 146.52 - x = 146.70 gives x = 146.34 MHz, and 2x - 146.52 = 146.70 gives x = 146.61 MHz. So the two likely interfering frequencies are 146.34 MHz and 146.61 MHz.
A-005-006-005: A phase modulator varies the tuning (reactance) of an amplifier's tank circuit to shift the signal's phase, which produces FM (indirect FM). Changing the tank reactance changes the instantaneous frequency. A balanced modulator or mixer would not create FM this way.
A-005-006-006: A pre-emphasis network is added at the FM transmitter to boost the higher audio frequencies relative to the lower ones before modulation, which improves the high-frequency signal-to-noise ratio. The receiver then applies the opposite de-emphasis to restore natural balance and cut high-frequency noise. De-emphasis is the receiver-side complement, so the transmitter network is pre-emphasis.
A-005-006-007: A 2-metre repeater duplexer needs cavity filters: high-Q resonant cavities provide the very sharp, low-loss selectivity required to let transmit and receive share one antenna without the transmitter swamping the receiver. L-C, crystal, and DSP filters cannot give that combination of low loss and steep skirts at VHF power levels. Cavities are the standard choice.
A-005-006-008: Phase modulation and direct frequency modulation differ in how deviation varies with audio frequency, and the practical difference shows up as pre-emphasis: phase modulation inherently emphasizes higher audio frequencies, equivalent to the pre-emphasis applied to true FM. So pre-emphasis characterizes the distinction. Centre frequency and inversion are unrelated.
A-005-006-009: A compressor and clipper are placed between the audio amplifier and the modulator so the audio is level-controlled and peak-limited before it modulates the carrier, improving talk power and preventing overdeviation. They must act on the audio signal path ahead of modulation. Placing them after the modulator or oscillator would be too late.
A-005-006-010: For an FM transmitter the three key parameters to verify are power output, frequency deviation, and frequency stability, ensuring it is on frequency, not overdeviating, and at legal power. These directly affect signal quality and interference. Carrier suppression and sideband power are SSB/AM concerns, not FM.
A-005-006-011: Intermodulation products come from non-linear mixing in stages handling multiple strong signals, the final amplifier, the receiver front end, or passive junctions (passive intermod). The intermediate-frequency stage handles a single converted signal and is not where intermod typically originates. So the IF stage is the odd one out.
A-005-007-001: Automatic level control (ALC) holds the peak RF output of an SSB transmitter roughly constant by reducing gain when input peaks rise, preventing overdrive and flat-topping. It acts on the transmit chain. AGC, AOC, and AVC are receiver gain-control terms, not the transmit peak-limiting circuit.
A-005-007-002: Speech compression amplifies weak (low-level) speech fully while reducing or limiting amplification of strong (high-level) peaks, which raises average power and improves intelligibility without overdriving. The first option states this correctly. Compression actually improves the effective signal-to-noise of speech rather than lowering it.
A-005-007-003: A digital signal processor contains an analog-to-digital converter, a mathematical transform, and a digital-to-analog converter, but there is no such thing as an aliasing amplifier; aliasing is an unwanted artifact to be avoided, not a circuit block. So the aliasing amplifier is the item that does not belong. The other three are genuine DSP components.
A-005-007-004: The number of discrete levels is 2 raised to the number of bits, so for 256 levels you need n where 2ⁿ = 256, and 2⁸ = 256, giving 8 bits. Each added bit doubles the levels. 4 bits gives only 16 and 6 bits only 64, both too few.
A-005-007-005: Each added bit doubles the resolution, and doubling corresponds to about 6 dB (since 20 × log₁₀(2) ≈ 6 dB), so one more bit adds roughly 6 dB of dynamic range. This is the well-known 6 dB-per-bit rule for digitizers. The 1, 3, and 4 dB answers do not match the factor-of-two-per-bit relationship.
A-005-007-006: A circuit using an analog-to-digital converter, a mathematical transform, a digital-to-analog converter, and a low-pass (reconstruction) filter is a digital signal processor (DSP). These blocks digitize, process, and reconstruct the signal. The other names are not standard terms for this complete chain.
A-005-007-007: Frequency division is a digital/logic technique, not an analog signal-processing method, so it is the one not associated with analog processing. Compression, bandwidth limiting, and clipping all operate directly on analog waveforms. Hence frequency division is the odd one out.
A-005-007-008: Peak limiting in a speech processor is done by compression, AF (audio) clipping, or RF clipping, but there is no method called frequency clipping. Clipping flattens peaks in either the audio or RF domain, and compression rides the gain down on peaks. So frequency clipping is the non-existent method.
A-005-007-009: Clipping the audio waveform flattens its peaks, and those sharp corners are rich in harmonics, so AF clipping increases harmonic distortion that can fall outside the desired passband. That distortion is the main drawback. RF clipping followed by filtering reduces this problem, which is why RF clipping is generally preferred.
A-005-007-010: Compared with AF clipping, RF clipping is harder and more expensive to implement, but it produces less distortion because the resulting splatter can be filtered out at RF. The statement that it is easier to implement is therefore the incorrect description. Its advantage is lower distortion, not lower cost or complexity.
A-005-007-011: Automatic Level Control is essentially a form of RF compression: it reduces the transmitter's gain on RF peaks to hold output level constant and prevent overdrive. It acts on the RF/output level, not on the audio or by clipping. So ALC is another name for RF compression.
A-005-008-001: Varicode uses code elements of unequal length, assigning shorter codes to common characters and longer codes to rare ones (similar in spirit to Morse), which is why it is used in PSK31. Baudot and ASCII use fixed-length codes, and AX.25 is a packet protocol, not a variable-length character code. So Varicode is the variable-length code.
A-005-008-002: The base (lowest) layer of the OSI model is the physical layer, which covers the actual electrical/mechanical connection, here the TNC-to-terminal interconnection. Higher layers (link, network, transport) handle framing, routing, and end-to-end delivery. So the physical interconnection corresponds to the physical layer.
A-005-008-003: A Cyclic Redundancy Check appends a calculated value so the receiver can recompute it and detect whether the data was corrupted; its purpose is error detection. It flags bad frames but does not by itself correct them. CRC is not a compression method, so the compression answers are wrong.
A-005-008-004: ASCII's advantage over Baudot is its larger character set that includes both upper- and lower-case letters (plus more symbols), whereas 5-bit Baudot cannot. ASCII has no built-in error correction and uses more, not fewer, bits per character. So the upper/lower-case capability is the real advantage.
A-005-008-005: AMTOR ARQ (Mode A) is an automatic-repeat-request system: the receiving station detects errors and automatically requests retransmission of any bad characters. This handshaking gives reliable copy on a clear path between two stations. The other options describe FEC, packet FCS, or claim no error control, none of which match Mode A.
A-005-008-006: AMTOR FEC (Mode B) is forward error correction used for broadcasts, where each character is sent twice (time-diversity) so the receiver can recover it without a return channel. There is no acknowledgement path in FEC mode, unlike ARQ. So sending each character twice is the FEC method.
A-005-008-007: APRS supports two-way messaging, telemetry, and local information broadcasts, but it does not provide automatic link establishment (ALE), which is a separate HF technology for selecting usable frequencies. So automatic link establishment is the function APRS lacks. The other three are core APRS features.
A-005-008-008: A CRC is generated using a hash-type function (polynomial division) that condenses the message into a short check value. Huffman coding, convolution codes, and Lempel-Ziv are compression or error-correction techniques, not CRC generators. So a hash function is the algorithm behind a CRC.
A-005-008-009: AX.25 is the link-layer protocol used for amateur packet radio, defining how frames are addressed and exchanged. It is specifically the packet mode standard. RTTY, ASCII, and spread spectrum are unrelated modes, so packet is the correct association.
A-005-008-010: Baudot uses 5 information bits per character, giving 32 possible codes, which it extends with letters/figures shift to cover the full set. ASCII uses 7 and its extensions 8 bits. So Baudot's information-bit count is 5.
A-005-008-011: Standard ASCII uses 7 bits, and the ISO-8859 extension adds one more to make 8 information bits, doubling the character set to include accented and special characters. So ISO-8859 uses 8 bits. The 5-, 6-, and 7-bit answers describe Baudot or plain ASCII, not the extension.
A-005-009-001: Spread spectrum is the wideband technique in which the RF carrier is deliberately varied over a wide bandwidth according to a predetermined (pseudo-random) sequence known to both ends. Spreading the energy gives interference resistance and low detectability. The other terms describe unrelated modes, not this carrier-spreading scheme.
A-005-009-002: Frequency hopping is the spread-spectrum method where the carrier's centre frequency jumps among many channels many times per second following a pseudo-random list shared by transmitter and receiver. The carrier moves; it is not phase-shifted as in direct sequence. So changing channels per a pseudo-random list is frequency hopping.
A-005-009-003: Direct sequence spread spectrum uses a very fast pseudo-random binary bit stream to phase-shift (modulate) the RF carrier, spreading its energy across a wide band. The fast code, not channel hopping, does the spreading here. So the phase-shifting-by-fast-bitstream description is direct sequence.
A-005-009-004: Frequency hopping is a technique of spread spectrum transmission, where the carrier jumps among channels in a pseudo-random pattern. It is not part of AMTOR, packet, or RTTY, which are conventional narrowband modes. So frequency hopping belongs to spread spectrum.
A-005-009-005: Direct sequence is one of the spread spectrum methods, using a fast pseudo-random code to phase-modulate and spread the carrier. AMTOR, packet, and RTTY are narrowband digital modes, not spread spectrum. So direct sequence goes with spread spectrum.
A-005-009-006: Spread spectrum alters the carrier using a pseudo-random sequence that both transmitter and receiver know, so the receiver can despread and recover the signal. True random noise could not be reproduced at the receiver, so it cannot be used. The sequence must be pseudo-random (deterministic yet noise-like).
A-005-009-007: A spread-spectrum signal is hard to monitor because your receiver must be frequency- (and code-) synchronized to the transmitter's pseudo-random sequence to despread it; without that key the energy just looks like noise. The signal is wider than normal, not narrower, so that distractor is wrong. Synchronization to the spreading code is the barrier.
A-005-009-008: In frequency-hopping spread spectrum the carrier frequency is changed (hopped) among many channels following a pseudo-random list shared by both stations. The carrier moves in frequency rather than being amplitude- or phase-modulated to spread it. So the pseudo-random channel-changing description is correct.
A-005-009-009: In direct-sequence spread spectrum the carrier is phase-shifted by a fast pseudo-random binary bit stream, which spreads its energy across a wide band. The spreading comes from rapid phase modulation, not from hopping channels or amplitude/frequency companding. So the phase-shift-by-bitstream answer is correct.
A-005-009-010: Spread-spectrum reception is interference-resistant because the receiver despreads only signals that follow the correct spreading code; any signal not using that algorithm is spread out and suppressed during despreading, while the wanted signal is collapsed back to full strength. It is not about high power or special DSP filters. The matched-code processing is what rejects interference.
A-005-009-011: Frequency hopping works by changing the RF carrier frequency very rapidly according to a particular pseudo-random sequence known to both transmitter and receiver. The receiver hops in step to follow the signal. It does not rely on detecting interference and signalling the transmitter, which describes a different, adaptive scheme.
